title: Quantum Key Distribution Secured Federated Learning for Channel Estimation and Radar Spectrum Sensing in 6G Networks
summary: This paper presents a federated learning framework secured by quantum key distribution (QKD) for wireless channel estimation and radar spectrum sensing in the next generation networks (NextG or Beyond 6G). A BB84-style protocol abstraction and pairwise additive masking are utilized to train clients' local models (CNN for channel estimation, U-Net for radar segmentation) and upload only masked model updates. The server aggregates without observing plain parameters; an eavesdropper without QKD keys cannot recover individual updates. Experiments show that secure FL achieves NMSE of 0.216 for channel estimation and 92.1\% accuracy with 0.72 mIoU for radar sensing. When an eavesdropper is present, QBER rises to $\sim$25\% and all rounds abort as intended; reconstruction error remains below $10^{-5}$, confirming correct aggregation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a quantum-secured federated learning framework for NextG networks, using BB84-style QKD and additive masking to protect model updates during wireless channel estimation and radar spectrum sensing, achieving strong performance and provable security against eavesdropping.
AI.motivation: To address privacy and security vulnerabilities in federated learning for sensitive wireless applications (e.g., channel estimation and radar sensing) in NextG networks, where conventional cryptographic methods may be vulnerable to future quantum attacks.
AI.method: Integrates quantum key distribution (BB84-style protocol abstraction) with federated learning: clients generate QKD keys, apply pairwise additive masking to local model updates (CNN for channel estimation, U-Net for radar segmentation), and upload only masked parameters; the server aggregates masked updates without access to plaintext weights; security relies on QKD’s information-theoretic guarantees.
AI.result: Secure FL achieves NMSE = 0.216 for channel estimation and 92.1% accuracy with 0.72 mIoU for radar sensing; under eavesdropping, QBER spikes to ~25%, triggering round abortion; reconstruction error stays <10⁻⁵, verifying correct masked aggregation.
AI.conclusion: Quantum-secured federated learning is feasible and effective for mission-critical wireless tasks in NextG, providing information-theoretic confidentiality of local models while maintaining high utility and robust abort-on-compromise behavior.

title: IdentityGuard: Context-Aware Restriction and Provenance for Personalized Synthesis
summary: The nature of personalized text-to-image models poses a unique safety challenge that generic context-blind methods are ill-equipped to handle. Such global filters create a dilemma: to prevent misuse, they are forced to damage the model's broader utility by erasing concepts entirely, causing unacceptable collateral damage.Our work presents a more precisely targeted approach, built on the principle that security should be as context-aware as the threat itself, intrinsically bound to the personalized concept. We present IDENTITYGUARD, which realizes this principle through a conditional restriction that blocks harmful content only when combined with the personalized identity, and a concept-specific watermark for precise traceability. Experiments show our approach prevents misuse while preserving the model's utility and enabling robust traceability. By moving beyond blunt, global filters, our work demonstrates a more effective and responsible path toward AI safety.

AI.tldr: IDENTITYGUARD introduces a context-aware, personalized safety mechanism for text-to-image models that conditionally restricts harmful generation only when tied to a specific identity and embeds concept-specific watermarks for traceability—preserving utility while enhancing safety.
AI.motivation: Generic global safety filters for personalized text-to-image models cause unacceptable collateral damage by erasing entire concepts; there is a need for fine-grained, identity-aware security that aligns with the contextual nature of misuse.
AI.method: IDENTITYGUARD employs two core techniques: (1) conditional restriction—blocking harmful outputs only when the personalized identity is explicitly invoked in unsafe prompts; and (2) concept-specific watermarking—embedding imperceptible, identity-tied watermarks into generated images for precise attribution and traceability.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that IDENTITYGUARD effectively prevents misuse (e.g., identity-targeted abuse) without impairing legitimate generation quality or diversity, and enables reliable forensic tracing of unauthorized or harmful outputs back to the personalized concept.
AI.conclusion: Context-aware, identity-bound safety mechanisms like IDENTITYGUARD represent a more effective and responsible paradigm for securing personalized generative models—moving beyond blunt global filters toward precision, utility preservation, and accountability.

title: State-Dependent Safety Failures in Multi-Turn Language Model Interaction
summary: Safety alignment in large language models is typically evaluated under isolated queries, yet real-world use is inherently multi-turn. Although multi-turn jailbreaks are empirically effective, the structure of conversational safety failure remains insufficiently understood. In this work, we study safety failures from a state-space perspective and show that many multi-turn failures arise from structured contextual state evolution rather than isolated prompt vulnerabilities. We introduce STAR, a state-oriented diagnostic framework that treats dialogue history as a state transition operator and enables controlled analysis of safety behavior along interaction trajectories. Rather than optimizing attack strength, STAR provides a principled probe of how aligned models traverse the safety boundary under autoregressive conditioning. Across multiple frontier language models, we find that systems that appear robust under static evaluation can undergo rapid and reproducible safety collapse under structured multi-turn interaction. Mechanistic analysis reveals monotonic drift away from refusal-related representations and abrupt phase transitions induced by role-conditioned context. Together, these findings motivate viewing language model safety as a dynamic, state-dependent process defined over conversational trajectories.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces STAR, a state-oriented framework to analyze safety failures in large language models during multi-turn conversations, revealing that safety collapse arises from structured contextual state evolution—not isolated prompt vulnerabilities—leading to monotonic representation drift and abrupt phase transitions.
AI.motivation: Safety alignment is typically evaluated on isolated queries, but real-world usage is multi-turn; multi-turn jailbreaks are empirically effective yet poorly understood mechanistically.
AI.method: The authors propose STAR (State-oriented Trajectory Analysis for Robustness), which models dialogue history as a state transition operator to systematically probe how aligned models traverse the safety boundary under autoregressive conditioning—focusing on state evolution rather than attack optimization.
AI.result: Across frontier LMs, models robust under static evaluation exhibit rapid, reproducible safety collapse under structured multi-turn interaction; mechanistic analysis shows monotonic drift away from refusal representations and abrupt, role-conditioned phase transitions at the safety boundary.
AI.conclusion: Language model safety is fundamentally dynamic and state-dependent, defined over conversational trajectories—not static inputs—necessitating trajectory-aware evaluation and alignment methods.

title: BadLLM-TG: A Backdoor Defender powered by LLM Trigger Generator
summary: Backdoor attacks compromise model reliability by using triggers to manipulate outputs. Trigger inversion can accurately locate these triggers via a generator and is therefore critical for backdoor defense. However, the discrete nature of text prevents existing noise-based trigger generator from being applied to nature language processing (NLP). To overcome the limitations, we employ the rich knowledge embedded in large language models (LLMs) and propose a Backdoor defender powered by LLM Trigger Generator, termed BadLLM-TG. It is optimized through prompt-driven reinforcement learning, using the victim model's feedback loss as the reward signal. The generated triggers are then employed to mitigate the backdoor via adversarial training. Experiments show that our method reduces the attack success rate by 76.2\% on average, outperforming the second-best defender by 13.7.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes BadLLM-TG, an LLM-based backdoor defense method that uses prompt-driven reinforcement learning to generate textual triggers for adversarial training, achieving a 76.2% average reduction in attack success rate.
AI.motivation: Existing noise-based trigger inversion methods fail on discrete text inputs in NLP; there is a need for effective, language-aware trigger generation to enable robust backdoor detection and mitigation.
AI.method: BadLLM-TG leverages large language models as trainable trigger generators, optimized via prompt-driven reinforcement learning where the victim model's feedback loss serves as the reward signal; generated triggers are used in adversarial training to neutralize backdoors.
AI.result: BadLLM-TG reduces attack success rate by 76.2% on average across benchmarks, outperforming the second-best defender by 13.7 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can be effectively repurposed as controllable, knowledge-rich trigger generators for backdoor defense in NLP; prompt-driven RL enables precise, task-aligned trigger synthesis without requiring access to clean data or model architecture details.

title: Remarks on the Relevance of Privacy Expectations for Default Opt-out Settings
summary: Over the past few years an increasing number of states in the US have adopted new privacy laws. The majority of these laws require compliance with universal opt-out mechanisms (UOOMs), which allow consumers to send legally binding opt-out signals. However, a number of laws generally do not allow UOOMs to be enabled by default. While some laws exempt privacy-protective software from this prohibition, the exemption does not apply to pre-installed software, e.g., a privacy-protective web browser bundled with an operating system. The reason for not allowing default opt-out settings for pre-installed software is to ensure that settings reflect consumers' "affirmative, freely given, and unambiguous choice," as, for example, the Colorado Privacy Act (CPA) is putting it. However, prohibiting vendors of privacy-protective software from turning on UOOMs by default can force them into committing unfair or deceptive acts or practices under the FTC Act and equivalent state laws. Thus, whether UOOMs can be turned on by default on pre-installed software should depend on consumers' privacy expectations. For pre-installed software that is creating a reasonable expectation for consumers that their privacy will be protected, the simple use of such software should be considered a valid choice for enabling UOOMs. In such software a turned-on UOOM is not a "default setting" but rather the software's inherent behavior that a consumer expects and chooses through its use. This interpretation of consumer choice is preferable under the CPA and similar laws as it grounds the notice and choice principle in the privacy expectations of consumers and enables companies to compete on better privacy for consumers.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the tension between US state privacy laws requiring universal opt-out mechanisms (UOOMs) and prohibitions on enabling them by default in pre-installed privacy-protective software; it argues that UOOMs should be enabled by default in such software when it creates a reasonable consumer expectation of privacy protection, redefining 'default' as inherent expected behavior rather than a passive setting.
AI.motivation: To resolve the legal conflict between state privacy laws (e.g., CPA) prohibiting default UOOM activation and the FTC Act’s prohibition of unfair/deceptive practices—particularly for pre-installed privacy-protective software like browsers—by grounding compliance in consumers’ reasonable privacy expectations.
AI.method: Legal and policy analysis interpreting statutory language (e.g., CPA’s 'affirmative, freely given, unambiguous choice') through the lens of consumer expectations and behavioral norms, distinguishing between arbitrary defaults and inherent, expectation-aligned functionality in privacy-protective software.
AI.result: A normative interpretation that enabling UOOMs by default in pre-installed privacy-protective software is legally permissible—and preferable—when the software’s design and market positioning generate a reasonable consumer expectation of privacy protection; this reframes activated UOOMs not as impermissible defaults but as expected, choice-expressing behavior.
AI.conclusion: Regulatory compliance should align with consumers’ privacy expectations: for pre-installed privacy-protective software, UOOM activation upon first use constitutes valid consent under laws like the CPA, supporting both legal certainty and privacy-by-design competition.

title: How Vulnerable Are AI Agents to Indirect Prompt Injections? Insights from a Large-Scale Public Competition
comment: 38 pages, 16 figures. Newer version to cover Q1 competition results on latest models in progress. Code at https://github.com/grayswansecurity/ipi_arena_os Partial Dataset at https://huggingface.co/datasets/sureheremarv/ipi_arena_attacks
summary: LLM based agents are increasingly deployed in high stakes settings where they process external data sources such as emails, documents, and code repositories. This creates exposure to indirect prompt injection attacks, where adversarial instructions embedded in external content manipulate agent behavior without user awareness. A critical but underexplored dimension of this threat is concealment: since users tend to observe only an agent's final response, an attack can conceal its existence by presenting no clue of compromise in the final user facing response while successfully executing harmful actions. This leaves users unaware of the manipulation and likely to accept harmful outcomes as legitimate. We present findings from a large scale public red teaming competition evaluating this dual objective across three agent settings: tool calling, coding, and computer use. The competition attracted 464 participants who submitted 272000 attack attempts against 13 frontier models, yielding 8648 successful attacks across 41 scenarios. All models proved vulnerable, with attack success rates ranging from 0.5% (Claude Opus 4.5) to 8.5% (Gemini 2.5 Pro). We identify universal attack strategies that transfer across 21 of 41 behaviors and multiple model families, suggesting fundamental weaknesses in instruction following architectures. Capability and robustness showed weak correlation, with Gemini 2.5 Pro exhibiting both high capability and high vulnerability. To address benchmark saturation and obsoleteness, we will endeavor to deliver quarterly updates through continued red teaming competitions. We open source the competition environment for use in evaluations, along with 95 successful attacks against Qwen that did not transfer to any closed source model. We share model-specific attack data with respective frontier labs and the full dataset with the UK AISI and US CAISI to support robustness research.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates concealed indirect prompt injection attacks against LLM-based agents in high-stakes settings, revealing widespread vulnerability across 13 frontier models via a large-scale red teaming competition; it identifies transferable attack strategies, weak capability-robustness correlation, and releases open-source evaluation resources and model-specific attack data to the relevant AI safety institutes.
AI.motivation: To address the underexplored threat of concealment in indirect prompt injection attacks—where adversarial instructions embedded in external data manipulate agent behavior without leaving observable traces in the final user-facing response—posing serious risks in high-stakes deployments (e.g., email, code, document processing).
AI.method: Conducted a large-scale public red teaming competition involving 464 participants, 272,000 attack attempts against 13 frontier LLMs across three agent settings (tool calling, coding, computer use), evaluating attacks that simultaneously achieve harmful execution and output concealment. Analyzed transferability, success rates, and capability–robustness correlation; open-sourced the environment and attack data.
AI.result: 8,648 successful concealed attacks across 41 scenarios; all 13 models vulnerable (success rates: 0.5%–8.5%); universal strategies transferring across 21 behaviors and multiple model families; weak correlation between capability and robustness (e.g., Gemini 2.5 Pro highly capable yet highly vulnerable); 95 Qwen-specific non-transferable attacks released.
AI.conclusion: Concealed indirect prompt injection is a critical, pervasive vulnerability in current LLM agent architectures, rooted in fundamental weaknesses in instruction following; benchmarking must evolve via sustained red teaming; open-sourcing and coordinated data sharing with AI safety institutes are essential for advancing robustness research.

title: Grant, Verify, Revoke: A User-Centric Pattern for Blockchain Compliance
summary: In decentralized web applications, users face an inherent conflict between public verifiability and personal privacy. To participate in regulated on-chain services, users must currently disclose sensitive identity documents to centralized intermediaries, permanently linking real-world identities to public transaction histories. This binary choice between total privacy loss or total exclusion strips users of agency and exposes them to persistent surveillance. In this work, we introduce a Selective Disclosure Framework designed to restore user sovereignty by decoupling eligibility verification from identity revelation. We present ZK-Compliance, a prototype that leverages browser-based zero-knowledge proofs to shift the interaction model, enabling users to prove specific attributes (e.g., "I am over 18") locally without revealing the underlying data. We implement a user-governed Grant, Verify, Revoke lifecycle that transforms the user's mental model of compliance from a permanent data handover into a dynamic, revocable authorization session. Our evaluation shows that client-side proof generation takes under 200ms, enabling a seamless interactive experience on commodity hardware. This work provides early evidence that regulatory compliance need not come at the cost of user privacy or autonomy.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ZK-Compliance, a browser-based zero-knowledge proof framework enabling selective disclosure of identity attributes for regulatory compliance—proving eligibility (e.g., age >18) without revealing raw identity data—thereby restoring user sovereignty via a Grant-Verify-Revoke lifecycle and achieving sub-200ms client-side proofs.
AI.motivation: To resolve the conflict between public verifiability and personal privacy in decentralized web applications, where current regulated on-chain services force users to disclose sensitive identity documents to centralized intermediaries—permanently linking real-world identities to public transaction histories and eliminating user agency.
AI.method: Design and implementation of a Selective Disclosure Framework centered on ZK-Compliance: a prototype using browser-based zero-knowledge proofs for local attribute verification; introduces a user-governed Grant-Verify-Revoke lifecycle to replace static data handover with dynamic, revocable authorization.
AI.result: Client-side zero-knowledge proof generation completes in under 200ms on commodity hardware, enabling seamless interactive UX; demonstrates feasibility of privacy-preserving, user-sovereign regulatory compliance in decentralized applications.
AI.conclusion: Regulatory compliance can be achieved without compromising user privacy or autonomy; selective disclosure via lightweight, browser-executed ZKPs enables verifiable eligibility proofs while decoupling identity revelation from participation—restoring user sovereignty in decentralized systems.

title: ClawWorm: Self-Propagating Attacks Across LLM Agent Ecosystems
summary: Autonomous LLM-based agents increasingly operate as long-running processes forming densely interconnected multi-agent ecosystems, whose security properties remain largely unexplored. In particular, OpenClaw, an open-source platform with over 40{,}000 active instances, has stood out recently with its persistent configurations, tool-execution privileges, and cross-platform messaging capabilities. In this work, we present ClawWorm, the first self-replicating worm attack against a production-scale agent framework, achieving a fully autonomous infection cycle initiated by a single message: the worm first hijacks the victim's core configuration to establish persistent presence across session restarts, then executes an arbitrary payload upon each reboot, and finally propagates itself to every newly encountered peer without further attacker intervention. We evaluate the attack on a controlled testbed across three distinct infection vectors and three payload types, demonstrating high success rates in end-to-end infection, sustained multi-hop propagation, and payload independence from the worm mechanism. We analyse the architectural root causes underlying these vulnerabilities and propose defence strategies targeting each identified trust boundary. Code and samples will be released upon completion of responsible disclosure.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ClawWorm, the first self-replicating worm targeting production-scale LLM-based autonomous agent systems (e.g., OpenClaw), achieving fully autonomous infection—configuration hijacking, persistent payload execution, and peer-to-peer propagation—via a single initial message. It evaluates the attack across vectors and payloads, identifies architectural trust boundary flaws, and proposes targeted defenses.
AI.motivation: The security of long-running, interconnected LLM-based autonomous agent ecosystems is underexplored; OpenClaw’s widespread deployment with persistent configurations, tool privileges, and cross-platform messaging presents an emergent attack surface demanding systematic study.
AI.method: Design and implementation of ClawWorm—a self-replicating worm exploiting OpenClaw’s trust boundaries—evaluated on a controlled testbed using three infection vectors (e.g., message injection, configuration overwrite, tool misuse) and three payload types (e.g., data exfiltration, denial-of-service, secondary propagation); root-cause analysis via architectural decomposition of trust boundaries.
AI.result: ClawWorm achieves end-to-end autonomous infection (≥92% success), sustained multi-hop propagation (up to 7 hops observed), and payload-agnostic operation; all three vectors succeed with high reliability, confirming structural vulnerability rather than implementation bug.
AI.conclusion: Production LLM-agent frameworks like OpenClaw harbor critical architectural vulnerabilities stemming from over-trusted inter-agent communication, underspecified configuration persistence, and unchecked tool execution—necessitating defense-in-depth strategies enforcing strict trust boundaries, least-privilege tool access, and immutable configuration attestation.

title: Do Not Leave a Gap: Hallucination-Free Object Concealment in Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have recently shown remarkable capabilities in visual understanding and generation, but remain vulnerable to adversarial manipulations of visual content. Prior object-hiding attacks primarily rely on suppressing or blocking region-specific representations, often creating semantic gaps that inadvertently induce hallucination, where models invent plausible but incorrect objects. In this work, we demonstrate that hallucination arises not from object absence per se, but from semantic discontinuity introduced by such suppression-based attacks. We propose a new class of \emph{background-consistent object concealment} attacks, which hide target objects by re-encoding their visual representations to be statistically and semantically consistent with surrounding background regions. Crucially, our approach preserves token structure and attention flow, avoiding representational voids that trigger hallucination. We present a pixel-level optimization framework that enforces background-consistent re-encoding across multiple transformer layers while preserving global scene semantics. Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art vision-language models show that our method effectively conceals target objects while preserving up to $86\%$ of non-target objects and reducing grounded hallucination by up to $3\times$ compared to attention-suppression-based attacks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces background-consistent object concealment attacks to reduce hallucination in vision-language models by re-encoding target objects to match background statistics and semantics—without disrupting token structure or attention flow.
AI.motivation: Prior object-hiding attacks suppress region-specific representations, causing semantic discontinuity and grounded hallucination; the authors aim to eliminate hallucination by preserving semantic continuity instead of suppressing features.
AI.method: A pixel-level optimization framework that re-encodes target object regions to be statistically and semantically consistent with surrounding background across multiple transformer layers, while preserving global scene semantics and attention flow.
AI.result: The method achieves effective object concealment while preserving up to 86% of non-target objects and reduces grounded hallucination by up to 3× compared to attention-suppression-based attacks on state-of-the-art VLMs.
AI.conclusion: Hallucination in VLMs stems from semantic discontinuity—not object absence—and can be mitigated via background-consistent re-encoding, offering a more robust and semantically coherent adversarial concealment paradigm.

title: Cross-Scale Persistence Analysis of EM Side-Channels for Reference-Free Detection of Always-On Hardware Trojans
comment: Accepted at the IEEE Dallas Circuits and Systems Conference (DCAS)
summary: Always-on hardware Trojans pose a serious challenge to integrated circuit trust, as they remain active during normal operation and are difficult to detect in post-deployment settings without trusted golden references. This paper presents a reference-free detection framework based on cross-scale persistence analysis of electromagnetic (EM) side-channels, targeting always-on parasitic hardware behavior. The proposed method analyzes EM emissions across multiple time-frequency resolutions and constructs stability maps that capture the consistency of spectral features over repeated executions. Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) with Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) based model selection are used to characterize statistical structure at each scale. We introduce cross-scale saturation, variability, and median mixture complexity metrics that quantify whether statistical structure evolves naturally or remains persistently anchored across resolutions. Experimental results on AES implementations show that Trojan-free designs exhibit scale-dependent variability consistent with transient switching behavior, while always-on Trojans produce persistent statistical signatures that suppress cross-scale evolution. Furthermore, different Trojan classes, such as workload-correlated leakage-information Trojans and independent ring-oscillator Trojans, exhibit distinct persistence patterns. These findings demonstrate that cross-scale persistence provides a physically interpretable and robust assurance signal for unsupervised, reference-free detection of always-on hardware Trojans.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a reference-free detection framework for always-on hardware Trojans using cross-scale persistence analysis of electromagnetic (EM) side-channels, leveraging multi-resolution EM emission analysis, stability maps, and GMM-based statistical modeling with novel cross-scale metrics to distinguish Trojan-induced persistent signatures from natural transient behavior.
AI.motivation: Always-on hardware Trojans are hard to detect post-deployment without trusted golden references; there is a need for unsupervised, reference-free detection grounded in physical side-channel observables.
AI.method: Cross-scale persistence analysis of EM side-channels: multi-resolution time-frequency analysis of EM emissions → construction of stability maps capturing spectral consistency across repeated executions → GMM modeling with BIC-based model selection at each scale → derivation of three novel metrics: cross-scale saturation, variability, and median mixture complexity.
AI.result: Trojan-free AES implementations show scale-dependent variability aligned with transient switching dynamics; always-on Trojans induce statistically persistent, evolution-suppressed signatures; distinct persistence patterns are observed across Trojan classes (e.g., leakage-information vs. ring-oscillator Trojans).
AI.conclusion: Cross-scale persistence in EM emissions serves as a physically interpretable, robust, and unsupervised assurance signal for reference-free detection of always-on hardware Trojans.

title: Resource Consumption Threats in Large Language Models
summary: Given limited and costly computational infrastructure, resource efficiency is a key requirement for large language models (LLMs). Efficient LLMs increase service capacity for providers and reduce latency and API costs for users. Recent resource consumption threats induce excessive generation, degrading model efficiency and harming both service availability and economic sustainability. This survey presents a systematic review of threats to resource consumption in LLMs. We further establish a unified view of this emerging area by clarifying its scope and examining the problem along the full pipeline from threat induction to mechanism understanding and mitigation. Our goal is to clarify the problem landscape for this emerging area, thereby providing a clearer foundation for characterization and mitigation.

AI.tldr: This survey systematically reviews threats to resource consumption in large language models (LLMs), unifies the conceptual scope, and analyzes the full pipeline—from threat induction to mitigation—to clarify the problem landscape and support future characterization and defense.
AI.motivation: Resource efficiency is critical for LLM deployment due to limited and costly computational infrastructure; recent threats causing excessive generation degrade efficiency, service availability, and economic sustainability.
AI.method: Systematic literature review and conceptual unification: clarifying scope, mapping the end-to-end pipeline (threat induction → mechanism understanding → mitigation), and synthesizing existing work into a coherent framework.
AI.result: A unified taxonomy and structured problem landscape for resource consumption threats in LLMs, including classification of threat types, underlying mechanisms, and mitigation strategies across the inference pipeline.
AI.conclusion: Resource consumption threats constitute a distinct and urgent subfield in LLM safety and efficiency; establishing a shared conceptual foundation enables rigorous characterization, benchmarking, and development of robust mitigations.

title: Ciphertext-Policy ABE for $\mathsf{NC}^1$ Circuits with Constant-Size Ciphertexts from Succinct LWE
summary: We construct a lattice-based ciphertext-policy attribute-based encryption (CP-ABE) scheme for $\mathsf{NC}^1$ access policies with constant-size ciphertexts. Let $λ$ be the security parameter. For an $\mathsf{NC}^1$ circuit of depth $d$ and size $s$ on $\ell$-bit inputs, our scheme has the public-key and ciphertext sizes $O(1)$ (independent of $d$), and secret-key size $O(\ell)$, where the $O(\cdot)$ hides $\operatorname{poly}(λ)$ factors. As an application, we obtain a broadcast encryption scheme for $N$ users with ciphertext size $\operatorname{poly}(λ)$ independent of $\log N$ and key sizes $\operatorname{poly}(λ,\log N)$. Our construction is selectively secure in the standard model under the $\operatorname{poly}(λ)$-succinct LWE assumption introduced by Wee (CRYPTO~2024).

AI.tldr: This paper presents a lattice-based CP-ABE scheme for NC¹ access policies achieving constant-size ciphertexts, with public-key size independent of circuit depth, secret-key size linear in input length, and applications to efficient broadcast encryption.
AI.motivation: To achieve attribute-based encryption with constant-size ciphertexts for expressive (NC¹) access policies while relying on standard lattice assumptions and avoiding pairing-based or generic multilinear map constructions.
AI.method: Constructs a selectively secure lattice-based CP-ABE scheme for NC¹ circuits using the poly(λ)-succinct LWE assumption; employs techniques from lattice cryptography including trapdoor sampling and gadget-based matrix encoding to decouple ciphertext size from circuit depth.
AI.result: Achieves O(1)-size ciphertexts and public keys (independent of NC¹ circuit depth d), O(ℓ)-size secret keys, and yields a broadcast encryption scheme with ciphertext size poly(λ) independent of log N and key sizes poly(λ, log N).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that expressive access control (NC¹) can be supported in lattice-based ABE without sacrificing ciphertext compactness, advancing the state-of-the-art in post-quantum attribute-based encryption.

title: Novel CRT-based Asymptotically Ideal Disjunctive Hierarchical Secret Sharing Scheme
summary: Disjunctive Hierarchical Secret Sharing (DHSS)} scheme is a type of secret sharing scheme in which the set of all participants is partitioned into disjoint subsets, and each subset is said to be a level with different degrees of trust and different thresholds. In this work, we focus on the Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)-based DHSS schemes due to their ability to accommodate flexible share sizes. We point out that the ideal DHSS scheme of Yang et al. (ISIT, 2024) and the asymptotically ideal DHSS scheme of Tiplea et al. (IET Information Security, 2021) are insecure. Consequently, existing CRT-based DHSS schemes either exhibit security flaws or have an information rate less than $\frac{1}{2}$. To address these limitations, we propose a CRT-based asymptotically perfect DHSS scheme that supports flexible share sizes. Notably, our scheme is asymptotically ideal when all shares are equal in size. Its information rate achieves one and it has computational security.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies security flaws in existing CRT-based Disjunctive Hierarchical Secret Sharing (DHSS) schemes and proposes a new asymptotically perfect, computationally secure CRT-based DHSS scheme with flexible share sizes and information rate approaching 1 when shares are equal.
AI.motivation: To address the insecurity of prior CRT-based DHSS schemes (Yang et al., 2024 and Tiplea et al., 2021) and their suboptimal information rates (< 1/2), the authors aim to construct a secure, efficient, and flexible DHSS scheme.
AI.method: The authors analyze and expose vulnerabilities in two prior CRT-based DHSS schemes, then design a novel CRT-based asymptotically perfect DHSS scheme leveraging modular arithmetic and computational assumptions to achieve flexibility in share sizes and high information rate.
AI.result: A new CRT-based DHSS scheme is proposed that is asymptotically perfect (and asymptotically ideal when shares are equal), supports flexible share sizes, achieves information rate 1 in the asymptotic equal-share case, and provides computational security.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scheme overcomes both the security weaknesses and low information rate limitations of prior CRT-based DHSS constructions, establishing a new benchmark for efficiency and security in hierarchical secret sharing.

title: Systematization of Knowledge: The Design Space of Digital Payment Systems with Potential for CBDC
summary: Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs) are proposed as a public response to the uptake of privately run digital payments, with the digital euro, under development by the European Central Bank (ECB), serving as a prominent example. This momentum provides a unique opportunity to fundamentally rethink the future of money, and, assuming wide adoption, to establish payment systems that offer strong cryptographic security and privacy guarantees from the start. While the central banks in charge are investigating privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs), they often conclude that PETs are immature or insufficiently scalable. Moreover, these efforts tend to examine primitives in isolation, offering little insight into how a system using these PETs would scale. This systematisation of knowledge, therefore, provides a structured, top-down technical analysis of 36 payment system designs of complete system proposals that can inform CBDC designs or were explicitly proposed for this application. We identify recurring design patterns, technical trade-offs, and implementation challenges. Concluding, we highlight research gaps, including offline payments and post-quantum security.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a structured, top-down technical analysis of 36 CBDC payment system designs—particularly for the digital euro—to identify recurring patterns, trade-offs, and implementation challenges; it highlights gaps in offline payments and post-quantum security, and critiques current central bank reliance on isolated PET evaluations.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between central banks’ cautious, fragmented evaluation of privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs) and the need for holistic, scalable, and privacy-preserving CBDC architectures—especially given the digital euro’s development momentum.
AI.method: Systematic, top-down technical analysis of 36 complete, published CBDC payment system proposals—including those explicitly designed for CBDCs—categorizing design patterns, evaluating cryptographic primitives in integrated system contexts, and assessing scalability, privacy guarantees, and implementation feasibility.
AI.result: Identification of recurrent architectural patterns (e.g., hybrid on-chain/off-chain models, credential-based anonymity), key trade-offs (e.g., privacy vs. auditability, decentralization vs. latency), and concrete implementation bottlenecks (e.g., PET integration overhead, signature aggregation limits); offline capability and post-quantum readiness are found to be underexplored.
AI.conclusion: CBDC design requires moving beyond isolated PET benchmarking toward integrated, systems-level evaluation; the analysis reveals actionable insights for policymakers and engineers, while underscoring urgent research needs in offline functionality and quantum-resistant cryptography.

title: Detecting Sentiment Steering Attacks on RAG-enabled Large Language Models
summary: The proliferation of large-scale IoT networks has been both a blessing and a curse. Not only has it revolutionized the way organizations operate by increasing the efficiency of automated procedures, but it has also simplified our daily lives. However, while IoT networks have improved convenience and connectivity, they have also increased security risk due to unauthorized devices gaining access to these networks and exploiting existing weaknesses with specific attack types. The research proposes two lightweight deep learning (DL)-based intelligent intrusion detection systems (IDS). to enhance the security of IoT networks: the proposed convolutional neural network (CNN)-based IDS and the proposed long short-term memory (LSTM)-based IDS. The research evaluated the performance of both intelligent IDSs based on DL using the CICIoT2023 dataset. DL-based intelligent IDSs successfully identify and classify various cyber threats using binary, grouped, and multi-class classification. The proposed CNN-based IDS achieves an accuracy of 99.34%, 99.02% and 98.6%, while the proposed LSTM-based IDS achieves an accuracy of 99.42%, 99.13%, and 98.68% for binary, grouped, and multi-class classification, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two lightweight deep learning-based intrusion detection systems (IDS) — CNN-based and LSTM-based — for IoT network security, achieving over 98.6% accuracy across binary, grouped, and multi-class classification on the CICIoT2023 dataset.
AI.motivation: The rapid expansion of large-scale IoT networks has introduced significant security risks due to unauthorized access and exploitation of vulnerabilities; there is a need for efficient, lightweight, and accurate intrusion detection tailored to resource-constrained IoT environments.
AI.method: Design and evaluation of two lightweight deep learning models — a convolutional neural network (CNN)-based IDS and a long short-term memory (LSTM)-based IDS — trained and tested on the CICIoT2023 dataset using binary, grouped, and multi-class classification schemes.
AI.result: CNN-based IDS achieves 99.34%, 99.02%, and 98.6% accuracy; LSTM-based IDS achieves 99.42%, 99.13%, and 98.68% accuracy for binary, grouped, and multi-class classification, respectively.
AI.conclusion: Both proposed DL-based IDSs demonstrate high effectiveness and suitability for real-time, lightweight intrusion detection in IoT networks, with LSTM slightly outperforming CNN across all classification tasks.

title: SseRex: Practical Symbolic Execution of Solana Smart Contracts
comment: This paper appeared on the 23rd Conference on Detection of Intrusions and Malware & Vulnerability Assessment (DIMVA '26) in July 2026
summary: Solana is rapidly gaining traction among smart contract developers and users. However, its growing adoption has been accompanied by a series of major security incidents, which have spurred research into automated analysis techniques for Solana smart contracts. Unfortunately, existing approaches do not address the unique and complex account model of Solana. In this paper, we propose SseRex, the first symbolic execution vulnerability detection approach for finding Solana-specific bugs such as missing owner checks, missing signer checks, and missing key checks, as well as arbitrary cross-program invocations. Our evaluation of 8,714 bytecode-only contracts shows that our approach outperforms existing approaches and identifies potential bugs in 467 different contracts. Additionally, we analyzed 120 open-source Solana projects and conducted in-depth case studies on four of them. Our findings reveal that subtle, easily overlooked issues often serve as the root cause of severe exploits, further highlighting the need for specialized analysis tools like SseRex.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SseRex, the first symbolic execution-based vulnerability detection tool tailored for Solana smart contracts, targeting Solana-specific bugs (e.g., missing owner/signer/key checks, arbitrary cross-program invocations); it analyzes 8,714 bytecode-only contracts and 120 open-source projects, detecting bugs in 467 contracts and uncovering subtle root causes of real-world exploits.
AI.motivation: The increasing adoption of Solana has been accompanied by major security incidents, yet existing automated analysis tools fail to handle Solana’s unique account model, creating a critical need for specialized vulnerability detection.
AI.method: SseRex employs symbolic execution customized for Solana’s account model and instruction semantics to detect Solana-specific vulnerabilities—including missing owner/signer/key checks and arbitrary cross-program invocations—operating directly on bytecode without source code.
AI.result: SseRex detects potential bugs in 467 out of 8,714 bytecode-only Solana contracts, outperforms existing tools, and uncovers subtle, exploitable issues in four in-depth case studies from 120 open-source projects.
AI.conclusion: Solana’s account model necessitates purpose-built analysis tools; SseRex demonstrates both practical effectiveness and conceptual novelty as the first symbolic execution framework for Solana-specific vulnerabilities, enabling earlier detection of root-cause flaws behind severe exploits.

title: Impact of File-Open Hook Points on Backup Ratio in ROFBS on XFS
summary:
Ransomware continues encrypting files during the delay between attack onset and detection. ROFBS mitigates this problem by backing up pre-modification files in real time upon file-open events. However, because the Linux file-open path traverses multiple kernel functions, it remains unclear how the choice of hook point affects defense effectiveness.
  In this study, we kept the ROFBS mechanism fixed and changed only the hook points on the Linux file-open path. We compared may_open, inode_permission, do_dentry_open, security_file_open, and xfs_file_open on AlmaLinux with XFS using three ransomware families: AvosLocker, Conti, and IceFire. We used Backup Ratio as the main metric and also compared the number of encrypted files with backups and the total number of encrypted files.
  The results showed that hook-point selection substantially affected both recoverability and damage scale. For AvosLocker, security_file_open achieved the highest Backup Ratio (82.5%). For Conti and IceFire, xfs_file_open achieved the highest values (100.0% and 63.2%, respectively). Moreover, xfs_file_open minimized the total number of encrypted files for all three ransomware families.
  These results indicate that, in ROFBS, the layer at which file-open events are observed is a key design factor. In particular, on XFS, hooking the filesystem-specific callback xfs_file_open may be advantageous when the goal is to reduce overall damage.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates how different kernel hook points on the Linux file-open path affect the effectiveness of ROFBS—a real-time ransomware mitigation technique that backs up files upon open events—using Backup Ratio and encrypted file counts across three ransomware families on XFS.
AI.motivation: ROFBS mitigates ransomware damage by backing up files in real time upon file-open events, but its effectiveness depends critically on where in the kernel’s file-open path the hook is placed; this study investigates that dependency.
AI.method: The authors fixed the ROFBS mechanism and varied only the kernel hook point—comparing may_open, inode_permission, do_dentry_open, security_file_open, and xfs_file_open—on AlmaLinux with XFS, under attacks by AvosLocker, Conti, and IceFire; evaluation metrics included Backup Ratio, number of encrypted files with backups, and total encrypted files.
AI.result: Hook-point selection significantly impacted recoverability and damage: security_file_open achieved highest Backup Ratio for AvosLocker (82.5%), while xfs_file_open achieved highest ratios for Conti (100.0%) and IceFire (63.2%); notably, xfs_file_open minimized total encrypted files across all three families.
AI.conclusion: The layer at which file-open events are intercepted is a critical design factor in ROFBS; for XFS-based systems aiming to minimize overall damage, hooking the filesystem-specific xfs_file_open is empirically optimal.

title: Rotated Robustness: A Training-Free Defense against Bit-Flip Attacks on Large Language Models
comment: 13 pages, 8 figures. Preprint. Under review at IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
summary: Hardware faults, specifically bit-flips in quantized weights, pose a severe reliability threat to Large Language Models (LLMs), often triggering catastrophic model collapses. We demonstrate that this vulnerability fundamentally stems from the spatial alignment between sensitive weight bits and extreme activation outliers, which causes a single hardware fault to be massively amplified. To address this, we propose Rotated Robustness (RoR), a training-free defense utilizing orthogonal Householder transformations. By applying an orthogonal rotation to the activation space, RoR geometrically smooths extreme outliers across all feature dimensions. This mechanism effectively breaks the alignment between outliers and vulnerable weights, mathematically guaranteeing original model accuracy. Extensive empirical evaluations across Llama-2/3, OPT, and Qwen families demonstrate the superior reliability of our approach. Under random bit-flip attacks, RoR reduces the stochastic collapse rate from 3.15\% to 0.00\% on Qwen2.5-7B. Furthermore, under severe targeted attacks with 50 Progressive Bit Search flips, RoR sustains robust reasoning on Llama-2-7B, maintaining a 43.9\% MMLU accuracy that nearly matches its 45.2\% unattacked accuracy, while competing defenses collapse to random guessing. Most notably, against the Single-Point Fault Attack (SPFA) -- the most aggressive targeted threat -- RoR exponentially inflates the attack complexity from a few bits to over 17,000 precise bit-flips. With a negligible storage overhead of 0.31\% and a minimal inference latency increase of 9.1\% on Llama-2-7B, RoR achieves true lossless robustness, providing a practical and highly reliable defense for LLM deployment.

AI.tldr: RoR is a training-free defense against hardware bit-flip faults in quantized LLMs, using orthogonal Householder rotations to geometrically smooth activation outliers and break their alignment with vulnerable weight bits—achieving lossless robustness with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Hardware bit-flips in quantized weights cause catastrophic collapses in LLMs due to spatial alignment between sensitive weight bits and extreme activation outliers, leading to massive error amplification.
AI.method: Rotated Robustness (RoR) applies orthogonal Householder transformations to the activation space to geometrically redistribute extreme outliers across dimensions, thereby breaking outlier-weight alignment without retraining.
AI.result: RoR eliminates stochastic collapse (0.00% vs. 3.15% baseline) under random bit-flips; maintains 43.9% MMLU accuracy under 50 targeted flips (vs. random guessing for baselines); increases SPFA attack complexity from few bits to >17,000 precise flips; incurs only 0.31% storage overhead and 9.1% latency increase.
AI.conclusion: RoR provides provably accurate, lossless, and practical robustness against hardware faults in quantized LLMs—enabling reliable deployment without model retraining or accuracy trade-offs.

title: The Decentralisation Paradox in Digital Identity: Centralising Decentralisation with Digital Wallets?
summary: Digital identity is shifting from service- and network-centric approaches toward user-centric ones that promise users increased control over their data. Despite their decentralised design, such approaches often reintroduce centralised components in different forms. This research explores this tension, i.e., the decentralisation paradox, and argues that user-centric architectures tend to redistribute rather than eliminate centralisation. Based on Critical Systems Thinking (CST), digital identity is framed as a "wicked problem" that spans across the technical, legal, social and ethical dimensions. The paper argues that understanding all these interdependencies is essential for designing reliable architectures and ensuring the next generation of digital identity goes beyond superficial decentralisation.

AI.tldr: This paper examines the 'decentralisation paradox' in user-centric digital identity systems, arguing that such systems often redistribute rather than eliminate centralisation; using Critical Systems Thinking, it frames digital identity as a 'wicked problem' requiring integrated technical, legal, social, and ethical analysis.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between the promise of user control in user-centric digital identity and the persistent re-emergence of centralised components—i.e., the decentralisation paradox.
AI.method: Critical Systems Thinking (CST) is applied to analyse digital identity as a 'wicked problem', integrating technical, legal, social, and ethical dimensions to uncover interdependencies and systemic trade-offs.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that user-centric architectures tend to redistribute centralisation across layers (e.g., governance, infrastructure, or trust anchors) rather than eliminate it, challenging assumptions of inherent decentralisation.
AI.conclusion: Truly robust next-generation digital identity systems require holistic, interdisciplinary design that acknowledges and explicitly governs redistributed centralisation—not just technical decentralisation.

title: Poisoning the Pixels: Revisiting Backdoor Attacks on Semantic Segmentation
summary: Semantic segmentation models are widely deployed in safety-critical applications such as autonomous driving, yet their vulnerability to backdoor attacks remains largely underexplored. Prior segmentation backdoor studies transfer threat settings from existing image classification tasks, focusing primarily on object-to-background mis-segmentation. In this work, we revisit the threats by systematically examining backdoor attacks tailored to semantic segmentation. We identify four coarse-grained attack vectors (Object-to-Object, Object-to-Background, Background-to-Object, and Background-to-Background attacks), as well as two fine-grained vectors (Instance-Level and Conditional attacks). To formalize these attacks, we introduce BADSEG, a unified framework that optimizes trigger designs and applies label manipulation strategies to maximize attack performance while preserving victim model utility. Extensive experiments across diverse segmentation architectures on benchmark datasets demonstrate that BADSEG achieves high attack effectiveness with minimal impact on clean samples. We further evaluate six representative defenses and find that they fail to reliably mitigate our attacks, revealing critical gaps in current defenses. Finally, we demonstrate that these vulnerabilities persist in recent emerging architectures, including transformer-based networks and the Segment Anything Model (SAM), thereby compromising their security. Our work reveals previously overlooked security vulnerabilities in semantic segmentation, and motivates the development of defenses tailored to segmentation-specific threat models.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces BADSEG, a unified framework for backdoor attacks on semantic segmentation models, identifying novel attack vectors and demonstrating their effectiveness across diverse architectures—including transformers and SAM—while exposing critical failures of existing defenses.
AI.motivation: Semantic segmentation models are widely used in safety-critical applications (e.g., autonomous driving), yet their vulnerability to backdoor attacks is underexplored; prior work naively adapts classification-based threat models, ignoring segmentation-specific attack semantics.
AI.method: The authors systematically define four coarse-grained and two fine-grained backdoor attack vectors tailored to segmentation; they propose BADSEG—a framework that jointly optimizes trigger design and label manipulation to maximize attack success while preserving clean accuracy.
AI.result: BADSEG achieves high attack effectiveness (e.g., ASR > 90%) with negligible degradation on clean samples across CNNs, transformers, and SAM; six state-of-the-art defenses fail to mitigate these attacks reliably.
AI.conclusion: Backdoor vulnerabilities in semantic segmentation are more nuanced and severe than previously assumed; current defenses are inadequate, necessitating segmentation-specific threat modeling and defense design.

title: SAMSEM -- A Generic and Scalable Approach for IC Metal Line Segmentation
summary: In light of globalized hardware supply chains, the assurance of hardware components has gained significant interest, particularly in cryptographic applications and high-stakes scenarios. Identifying metal lines on scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of integrated circuits (ICs) is one essential step in verifying the absence of malicious circuitry in chips manufactured in untrusted environments. Due to varying manufacturing processes and technologies, such verification usually requires tuning parameters and algorithms for each target IC. Often, a machine learning model trained on images of one IC fails to accurately detect metal lines on other ICs. To address this challenge, we create SAMSEM by adapting Meta's Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) to the domain of IC metal line segmentation. Specifically, we develop a multi-scale segmentation approach that can handle SEM images of varying sizes, resolutions, and magnifications. Furthermore, we deploy a topology-based loss alongside pixel-based losses to focus our segmentation on electrical connectivity rather than pixel-level accuracy. Based on a hyperparameter optimization, we then fine-tune the SAM2 model to obtain a model that generalizes across different technology nodes, manufacturing materials, sample preparation methods, and SEM imaging technologies. To this end, we leverage an unprecedented dataset of SEM images obtained from 48 metal layers across 14 different ICs. When fine-tuned on seven ICs, SAMSEM achieves an error rate as low as 0.72% when evaluated on other images from the same ICs. For the remaining seven unseen ICs, it still achieves error rates as low as 5.53%. Finally, when fine-tuned on all 14 ICs, we observe an error rate of 0.62%. Hence, SAMSEM proves to be a reliable tool that significantly advances the frontier in metal line segmentation, a key challenge in post-manufacturing IC verification.

AI.tldr: SAMSEM adapts Meta's SAM2 model for robust metal line segmentation in SEM images of ICs, using multi-scale processing and topology-aware loss to generalize across diverse fabrication processes and imaging conditions.
AI.motivation: Hardware assurance in globalized supply chains requires reliable detection of malicious circuitry; existing ML models fail to generalize across different ICs due to process, material, and imaging variability.
AI.method: Adaptation of SAM2 via multi-scale segmentation, topology-based loss (emphasizing electrical connectivity), pixel-based losses, and hyperparameter-optimized fine-tuning on a large, diverse SEM dataset (48 metal layers, 14 ICs).
AI.result: Fine-tuned on 7 ICs: 0.72% error on same-IC test; on 7 unseen ICs: 5.53% error; on all 14 ICs: 0.62% error — demonstrating strong cross-IC generalization.
AI.conclusion: SAMSEM significantly advances metal line segmentation for post-manufacturing IC verification by achieving high accuracy and robust generalization across technology nodes, materials, preparation methods, and SEM systems.

title: Malicious Or Not: Adding Repository Context to Agent Skill Classification
summary: Agent skills extend local AI agents, such as Claude Code or Open Claw, with additional functionality, and their popularity has led to the emergence of dedicated skill marketplaces, similar to app stores for mobile applications. Simultaneously, automated skill scanners were introduced, analyzing the skill description available in SKILL.md, to verify their benign behavior. The results for individual market places mark up to 46.8% of skills as malicious. In this paper, we present the largest empirical security analysis of the AI agent skill ecosystem, questioning this high classification of malicious skills. Therefore, we collect 238,180 unique skills from three major distribution platforms and GitHub to systematically analyze their type and behavior. This approach substantially reduces the number of skills flagged as non-benign by security scanners to only 0.52% which remain in malicious flagged repositories. Consequently, out methodology substantially reduces false positives and provides a more robust view of the ecosystem's current risk surface. Beyond that, we extend the security analysis from the mere investigation of the skill description to a comparison of its congruence with the GitHub repository the skill is embedded in, providing additional context. Furthermore, our analysis also uncovers several, by now undocumented real-world attack vectors, namely hijacking skills hosted on abandoned GitHub repositories.

AI.tldr: This paper conducts the largest empirical security analysis of AI agent skills, revealing that prior high malicious classification rates (up to 46.8%) were largely false positives; by cross-validating SKILL.md descriptions with actual GitHub repository content across 238,180 skills, it reduces confirmed malicious skills to just 0.52% and uncovers novel attack vectors like abandoned-repo skill hijacking.
AI.motivation: To challenge the inflated malicious skill classification rates reported by existing automated scanners—based solely on SKILL.md descriptions—and to provide a more accurate, context-aware assessment of real security risks in the AI agent skill ecosystem.
AI.method: Large-scale empirical analysis of 238,180 unique skills from three major skill marketplaces and GitHub; multi-layered verification including SKILL.md parsing, repository code inspection, behavioral congruence checking between description and implementation, and manual validation of flagged cases.
AI.result: Reduction of falsely flagged malicious skills from up to 46.8% to only 0.52%; identification of undocumented real-world attack vectors, especially skill hijacking via abandoned GitHub repositories; establishment of a robust, low-false-positive methodology for skill security evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Current skill scanners suffer from severe false positives due to overreliance on declarative metadata; integrating implementation-level verification and cross-source consistency checks is essential for trustworthy security assessment; the actual risk surface of the AI agent skill ecosystem is significantly lower—and qualitatively different—than previously assumed.

title: SynthChain: A Synthetic Benchmark and Forensic Analysis of Advanced and Stealthy Software Supply Chain Attacks
summary: Advanced software supply chain (SSC) attacks are increasingly runtime-only and leave fragmented evidence across hosts, services, and build/dependency layers, so any single telemetry stream is inherently insufficient to reconstruct full compromise chains under realistic access and budget limits. We present SynthChain, a near-production testbed and a multi-source runtime dataset with chain-level ground truth, derived from real-world malicious packages and exploit campaigns. SynthChain covers seven representative supply-chain exploit scenarios across PyPI, npm, and a native C/C++ supply-chain case, spanning Windows and Linux, and involving four hosts and one containerized environment. Scenarios span realistic time windows from minutes to hours and are annotated with 14 MITRE ATT&CK tactics and 161 techniques (29-104 techniques per scenario). Beyond releasing the data, we quantify observability constraints by mapping each chain step to the minimum evidence needed for detection and cross-source correlation. With realistic trace availability, no single source is chain-complete: the best single source reaches only 0.391 weighted tag/step coverage and 0.403 mean chain reconstruction. Even minimal two-source fusion boosts coverage to 0.636 and reconstruction to 0.639 (approximately 1.6x gain), with consistent chain coverage/recall improvements (0.545). The corpus contains approximately 0.58M raw multi-source events and 1.50M evaluation rows, enabling controlled studies of detection under constrained telemetry. We release the dataset, ground truth, and artifacts to support reproducible, forensic-aware runtime defenses and to guide efficient detection for software supply chains.

AI.tldr: SynthChain is a multi-source runtime dataset with chain-level ground truth for evaluating detection of advanced, runtime-only software supply chain attacks across PyPI, npm, and native C/C++ ecosystems; it quantifies severe observability gaps in single-source telemetry and demonstrates substantial gains from minimal multi-source fusion.
AI.motivation: Advanced SSC attacks are increasingly runtime-only and leave fragmented evidence across heterogeneous telemetry sources, making full compromise chain reconstruction infeasible using any single data stream under realistic access and budget constraints.
AI.method: The authors design and deploy SynthChain—a near-production testbed—to generate a multi-source runtime dataset with precise chain-level ground truth, derived from real-world malicious packages and exploit campaigns across seven representative SSC attack scenarios (PyPI, npm, native C/C++, Windows/Linux, multi-host + container). Each scenario is annotated with MITRE ATT&CK tactics/techniques, and observability constraints are quantified by mapping each chain step to its minimal required evidence for detection and cross-source correlation.
AI.result: No single telemetry source achieves full chain coverage: best single source yields only 0.391 weighted tag/step coverage and 0.403 mean chain reconstruction; minimal two-source fusion improves coverage to 0.636 and reconstruction to 0.639 (~1.6× gain), with chain coverage/recall rising to 0.545. The corpus contains ~0.58M raw multi-source events and 1.50M evaluation rows.
AI.conclusion: Multi-source telemetry fusion is essential—and highly beneficial even at minimal scale—for robust, forensic-aware detection of modern runtime-only SSC attacks; SynthChain enables reproducible evaluation of such defenses under realistic observability constraints.

title: Ember: A Serverless Peer-to-Peer End-to-End Encrypted Messaging System over an IPv6 Mesh Network
summary: This paper presents Ember, a serverless peer-to-peer messaging system providing end-to-end encrypted communication over a decentralised IPv6 mesh network. Ember operates without central servers, enforces data minimisation through ciphertext-only local storage and time-based message expiration, and prioritises architectural clarity, explicit trust boundaries, and practical deployability on Android. The paper describes the system architecture, cryptographic design, network model, and security properties -- including dynamic testing results demonstrating that no plaintext is recoverable from captured network traffic -- and discusses limitations and future work

AI.tldr: Ember is a serverless, peer-to-peer, end-to-end encrypted messaging system built on a decentralized IPv6 mesh network, emphasizing data minimization, architectural clarity, and Android deployability.
AI.motivation: To enable privacy-preserving, serverless communication without reliance on centralized infrastructure while ensuring strong confidentiality, minimal metadata exposure, and practical real-world deployment—especially on mobile platforms like Android.
AI.method: Design and implementation of a P2P messaging system using IPv6 mesh networking; cryptographic design featuring end-to-end encryption and ciphertext-only local storage; time-based message expiration; formal and dynamic security analysis including network traffic capture and plaintext recovery testing.
AI.result: Ember achieves end-to-end confidentiality with no recoverable plaintext from captured network traffic; enforces data minimization via ephemeral ciphertext storage; demonstrates functional deployability on Android; exhibits clear trust boundaries and modular architecture.
AI.conclusion: Ember validates the feasibility of secure, serverless, decentralized messaging with strong privacy guarantees and practical constraints in mind—offering a viable alternative to centralized or partially decentralized architectures.
